coredata.dc:title: A sensitive fluorescent assay for the determination of parathion-methyl using AHNSA probe with MnO2 nanosheets
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Bicheng
Peng, Zoujun
Wu, Sihao
He, Tianshuang
Qiu, Ping
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel fluorescence assay has been constructed for the determination of parathion-methyl (PM) by using 4-amino-3-hydroxy-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (AHNSA) as probe. MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NS) could quench the fluorescence of AHNSA, while Mn2+, the reduction product of MnO2 NS, has no influence on it, resulting in fluorescence recovery. This is because that MnO2 NS have oxidized characteristic, and they can react with choline (TCh), which is the product of acetylthiocholine (ATCh) catalyzed by acetylcholinesterase (AChE). In the presence of OPs, the activity of AChE was inhibited, accompanied by the restraint of the redox reaction of MnO2 NS, therefore the fluorescence of AHNSA was quenched. Under the optimized experimental conditions, a linear range of PM was determined to be 0.4–40 ng/mL (R2 = 0.997) by the proposed method with the limit of detection for 0.18 ng/mL (S/N = 3). The assay was successfully applied to the determination of PM in lake water, which average recoveries were between 86.5% and 114.4%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 4-Amino-3-hydroxy-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (AHNSA)
MnO2 nanosheets
Fluorescent assay
Organophosphorus pesticides

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of PM with MnO2 NS attaching AChE and ATCh
Characterization of MnO2 NS and AHNSA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of different systems. (a) AHNSA; (b) AChE + AHNSA; (c) ATCh + AHNSA; (d) AChE + ATCh + AHNSA; (e) AChE + ATCh + MnO2 NS + AHNSA; (f) AChE + PM + ATCh + MnO2 NS + AHNSA; (g) MnO2 NS + AHNSA.
(a) Zeta-potentials of MnO2 NS (b) Zeta-potentials of AHNSA.
(a) TEM image of MnO2 NS, (b) UV − vis absorption spectra of MnO2 NS, inset: digital image of MnO2 NS, (c) UV − vis absorption of AHNSA (35 μM) and (d) XPS spectra of MnO2 NS.
The factors of the concentration of AChE (a), pH (b), the concentration of MnO2 NS(c), the concentration of AHNSA(d), time (e) and the concentration of ATCh (f) affecting value (F0-F)/F0.

Sensitivity for determination of (a) PM (from up to down: 0.4, 4, 8, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, and 40 ng/mL) and (b) DDVP (from up to down: 0.5, 80, 120, 160 and 190 ng/mL), inset: calibration curve for PM and DDVP, respectively.
The detection mechanism for OPs by proposed assay.
Comparison of the proposed assay with other reported methods for the detection of pesticides.
Recovery test for PM in lake water samples.

head.ce:title: A sensitive fluorescent assay for the determination of parathion-methyl using AHNSA probe with MnO2 nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the presence of OPs, the activity of AChE was inhibited, accompanied by the restraint of the redox reaction of MnO2 NS, therefore the fluorescence of AHNSA was quenched.

In this paper, a novel fluorescence assay has been constructed for the determination of parathion-methyl (PM) by using 4-amino-3-hydroxy-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (AHNSA) as probe. MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NS) could quench the fluorescence of AHNSA, while Mn2+, the reduction product of MnO2 NS, has no influence on it, resulting in fluorescence recovery. This is because that MnO2 NS have oxidized characteristic, and they can react with choline (TCh), which is the product of acetylthiocholine (ATCh) catalyzed by acetylcholinesterase (AChE). In the presence of OPs, the activity of AChE was inhibited, accompanied by the restraint of the redox reaction of MnO2 NS, therefore the fluorescence of AHNSA was quenched. Under the optimized experimental conditions, a linear range of PM was determined to be 0.4–40 ng/mL (R2 = 0.997) by the proposed method with the limit of detection for 0.18 ng/mL (S/N = 3). The assay was successfully applied to the determination of PM in lake water, which average recoveries were between 86.5% and 114.4%.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphate pesticides (OPs) has been playing an important role in agricultural production since it came into being [1]. It is the most widely used pesticide in China which mainly includes chlorpyrifos, diazinon, fenthion, dichlorvos, parathion-methyl, malathion and pirimiphos-methyl, etc [2]. However, due to its widespread use, the residues in crops not only pollute the environment, but also threaten human health [3]. Therefore, it is fundamental to establish a fast, simple and low-cost detection method for pesticide residues in agricultural products [4].

Many scholars are committed to exploring the detection of pesticide residues. The main analytical methods are gas chromatography (GC) [5], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [7], electrochemical analysis [8] and enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [9]. However, these methods require highly skilled personnel, specially equipped laboratories, and expensive chemicals, which limit their widespread use in the determination of OPs residues [10]. Biosensors employed for the detection of pesticides/compounds proved to be more rapid, reliable, reproducible and convenient compared to the above-mentioned methods [11]. Because the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) can be irreversibly inhibited by OPs, many biosensors are constructed based on AChE and nanomaterials [12–14]. Various biosensors have been developed to detect OPs. For example, Liu et al. developed a dual-readout assay based on gold nanoparticles for organophosphorus and carbamate pesticides in complex solutions. By use of this dual-readout assay, the lowest detectable concentrations for several kinds of pesticides including carbaryl, diazinon, malathion, and phorate were measured to be 0.1, 0.1, 0.3, and 1 mg/L, respectively [15]. Zhang et al. developed an AChE inhibition biosensor based on a reduced graphene oxide/silver nanocluster/chitosan nanocomposite for detection of OPs, and the inhibition effect of phoxim was proportional to its concentration ranging from 0.2 to 250 nM with a detection limit of 81 pM [16]. Zhang et al. developed a colorimetric assay with polyacrylic acid-coated cerium oxide nanoparticles [17].

MnO2 nanomaterials have attracted the wide attention of many scientists because of their special photoelectric properties and excellent redox properties, and have been widely used in catalysis and electrode materials due to their high manganese reserves, low price and no pollution to the environment [18–22]. In recent years, the application of MnO2 nanomaterials in biosensors has been developed and has become a research hotspot. Lyu et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor with single excitation based MnO2 nanosheet for rapid and specific detection of ascorbic acid [23]. Halawa et al. developed fluorescent detection of glutathione based on lucigenin and MnO2 nanosheets [24]. Han et al. reported a fluorometric method for determination of H2O2, glucose and cholesterol by using MnO2 nanosheets modified with 5-carboxyfluorescein [25]. Yao et al reported a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for OPs detection based on opposite responses of two fluorescence reagents to MnO2 nanosheets [26]. As we know, TCh containing free thiol group is reductive and it can react with MnO2 NS [26]. In this paper, 4-amino-3-hydroxy-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (AHNSA) was chosen as a fluorescent agent because of its strong fluorescence intensity at 460 nm. However, its fluorescence happens to be quenched by MnO2 NS. MnO2 NS is reduced to Mn2+ by TCh, the hydrolyzed product of ACh. Once OPs is present, OPs irreversibly inhibits the activity of AChE. Based on this, we have proposed the fluorescence method using MnO2 NS combined with AChE and ATCh to detect OPs. Methyl parathion (PM) and 2,2-dichlorovinyl dimethyl phosphate (DDVP) [27]as two typical OPs used in this article.

Parathion-methyl (PM), 2,2-dichlorovinyl dimethyl phosphate (DDVP) was purchased from the Nation Institute of Metrology (Beijing, China) and used without further purification. The standard solution of PM, DDVP (0.01 mg/mL) were prepared by dissolving in methanol, respectively. Tetramethylammonium hydroxide and Tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane was purchased from Adamas-beta Reagent Company (Shanghai, China) and manganese chloride (MnCl2) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). 4-Amino-3-hydroxy-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (AHNSA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Acetylcholinesterase (AChE, 744 unit/mg) and acetylthiocholine iodide (ATCh) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). The buffer solutions used in this study as follows: Phosphate buffer (PB) (pH 5; 25 mM); Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH 8.5; 10 mM); Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.5; 20 mM) was used to dissolve the AChE (1 unit/mL). The solution of ATCh (10 mmol/L) was freshly prepared in ultrapure water, and used within 3 h. All chemicals were of analytical-reagent grade and the ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ, Millipore, USA) was used throughout the experiments.

UV–vis Absorption spectra were recorded using UV-2450 Shi-madzu Vis-spectrometer (Japan), The fluorescence spectra were performed on a F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), separately. Zeta potentials were obtained using a Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on a JEM-2100 (JEOL Co., Japan). The morphology of the MnO2 NS was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL Co., Japan) operating at 200 KV acceleration voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on Esca Lab 250Xi with Al K α X-ray radiation as the source for excitation. The binding energies were calibrated by C 1s (284.8 eV) and pass energy was 30.0 eV with spot size of 650 µm. The sample for TEM characterization was prepared by placing a drop of the MnO2 NS solution on a carbon-coated copper grid and drying it at room temperature.

MnO2 NS was synthesized by a simple wet chemical method [26]. For this, 10 mL tetramethylammonium hydroxide (6%) was added to 30 mL of H2O2 (3%), then 20 mL of MnCl2 (0.3 M) was quickly transferred into the above solution within 15 s. The color of the mixture solution quickly turned dark brown and the mixture was kept on stirring for 12 h at room temperature. After, the obtained mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min and washed thoroughly with ultrapure water and methanol. Next, the bulk MnO2 NS was dried at 60 °C for 72 h. 0.0030 g of MnO2 NS was Dissolved in 60 mL of water and sonicated it for 12 h to get the brown MnO2 NS dispersion. Finally, the brown solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for the further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PM with MnO2 NS attaching AChE and ATCh
ce:section.ce:para: AChE (50 μL, 100 U/L) and PM at different concentrations (10 μL) were added to centrifuge tubes and incubated at 37 °C for 0.5 h. ATCh (5 mM, 40 μL) and PBS buffer (200 μL, pH 8) were added to centrifuge tubes and incubated at 37 °C for another 0.5 h. MnO2 NS (50 μg/mL, 100 μL) and PB buffer (200 μL, pH 5) were added and then incubated for 25 min at room temperature. Then, AHNSA (20 μM, 10 μL) was added to the reaction system and diluted the final volume to 2000 μL. The fluorescence signal of the solution was recorded by fluorescence spectrometer.

As is shown in Scheme 1 , the whole system involves two reactions as following. Firstly, ATCh is catalyzed by AChE and hydrolyzed to form TCh and acetic acid. Secondly, TCh contained a sulfhydryl group (-SH) is reductive and can undergo redox reaction with MnO2 NS to form disulfide under appropriate conditions, accompanied by the reduction product for Mn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MnO2 NS and AHNSA
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, MnO2 NS was characterized by transmission electron microscopy. And it showed two-dimensional structure and exhibited multiple folds and crinkles in Fig. 3 a. MnO2 NS had a wide UV absorption band ranging from 300 nm to 700 nm and peaks were concentrated at 360 nm (Fig. 3b). Fig. 3c shows the absorption spectra of AHNSA with maximum absorbance at 340 nm. Detailed analysis based on Xray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further investigated to confirm the features of MnO2 NS. As shown in Fig. 3d, the binding energies of the XPS peaks of Mn2p3/2 and Mn2p1/2 located at 642.0 and 653.9 eV respectively, which were attributed to Mn2+ [28,29]. Thus, the above characterization indicated that MnO2 NS has been synthesized successfully.

For this, relative intensity was used to evaluate the optimal reaction system, which can be represented by (F0-F)/F0. Herein, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of AHNSA, and F is the fluorescence intensity after oxidation reaction. Firstly, we fixed the amount of ATCh at 5 mM, 100 μL, and changed the concentrations of AChE to get the maximum sensitivity. As shown in Fig. 4 a, with the change of AChE content, the sensitivity changed and the maximum sensitivity was obtained when the concentration of AChE was 250 U/L. As mentioned earlier, there were two reactions: the hydrolysis of ATCh and the redox reaction of MnO2 NS, so we optimized the pH of the two reaction steps. As shown in Fig. 4b, on account of the activity of the enzyme and oxidation of MnO2 NS, the maximum sensitivity was obtained when the pH was 8 in the hydrolysis of ATCh and the pH was 6 in the redox reaction of MnO2 NS. Next, the effect of concentrations of MnO2 NS (50–120 μg/mL) and AHNSA (5–35 μM) on relative intensities were investigated. It can be concluded from Fig. 4c and d that the optimal concentrations of MnO2 NS and AHNSA were 100 μg/mL and 30 μM, respectively. Then, we explored the effect of oxidation time of probe pairs. It was found that the maximum sensitivity was obtained after 25 min (Fig. 4e). The insufficiency of substrate could affect the oxidation of MnO2 NS, so the concentration of ATCh was investigated (Fig. 4f). With increasing the concentration of ATCh, the relative intensity raised consequently, and then kept constant after 5.0 mM ATCh. Therefore, 5.0 mM ATCh was used in the following experiment.

The linearity range and limit of detection (LOD) of the newly developed assay is evaluated for the determination of PM. In Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity reduced linearly with increasing of PM concentration from 0.4 to 40 ng/mL. The inset indicated a good fit to the linear model, and the obtained linear least square equation was expressed as Y = −2.68X + 1501.41 (ng/mL) with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.18 ng/mL. Herein, LOD = 3σ/k, where LOD stands for the detection limit, σ stands for the standard deviation, and k is the slope of the linear regression equation, respectively. To verify the feasibility of detection of other pesticides, DDVP was selected to analyze. The obtained linear least square equation was expressed as Y = −7.11X + 1659.32 (ng/mL) with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.993 and the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.24 ng/mL (Fig. 5b). The above results show that the method can be applied to the detection of organophosphorus pesticides. LOD and linear range of our assay was compared with the already reported methods, and the comparison is presented in Table 1 . It can be seen from Table 1 that the fluorescent assay for the determination of OPs using AHNSA probe with MnO2 nanosheets has higher sensitivity and superior properties than some other reported sensors [30–34].

To test the selectivity of the assay, the control experiment was performed based on several potential interfering ions in practical samples, including metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+ and Mg2+), anions (Cl-, SO4 2-, CO3 2– and PO4 3-), and biomolecules (Vitamin B (VB), glycine and tyrosine, humic acids, chlorophyll). We added the interfering substances to the reaction system in the presence of PM (20 ng/mL) and their final concentration were 100 times of the standard concentration of PM. Compared to the control group, fluorescence intensity ratio of the interferences is in the range from 0.95 to 1.05, so there have no significant difference (Fig. 6 ). In other words, these common substances do not interfere with the assay with high selectivity.

Lake water samples were collected from Longteng Lake and Qian Lake in Nanchang city, China. The water samples were pretreated according to the method reported in the previous literature [35]. The collected samples were filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane syringe filter to remove any particulate matters. Different concentrations of PM were added into the obtained samples and determined with the proposed assay. As summarized in Table 2 , the recovery of PM detection (recovery rate (%) = 100×(C detection amount/C addition amount)) in two water samples were in the range of 86.5–113.8% and 89.2–114.4% with RSD (n = 3) for less than 3.7% and 4.5%, respectively. The results demonstrated that the proposed methods can be applied to detect PM in practical samples.

In summary, a sensitive fluorescence assay for determination of organophosphorus pesticide was proposed using AHNSA probe with MnO2 nanosheets and acetylcholinesterase catalytic system for the first time. The proposed method shows sensitivity and high-selectivity towards PM, and the limit of detection reaches as low as 0.18 ng/mL. The strategy presented excellent selectivity for OPs detection, reducing the influence of the external environment, greatly improving the detection accuracy. The developed assay is quite simple in preparation and operation. Anyway, it may provide the potential applications in environmental analysis and food safety.

Bicheng Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Zoujun Peng: Validation, Visualization. Sihao Wu: Data curation. Tianshuang He: Investigation. Ping Qiu: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765015, 21808099) and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001), China.
